title: Supervised Learning Fundamentals
subtitle: C6 — Why models work, when they don't, and what "trained" actually means
markdown:
## Why this matters

Machine learning has two audiences in tech: people who *use* models (most engineers) and people who *build* them (a few). This page is for the first group — the engineer who needs to integrate a recommendation API, debug why the spam filter blocks legitimate emails, or have a real conversation with the data scientist on the team.

You don't need to do the math. You need to understand the *vocabulary* and the *failure modes*. That's the practical bar.

## The one-paragraph definition

Supervised learning means: you have **labeled examples** (input + correct output, repeated thousands of times), and you train a function that maps inputs to outputs. The function is the model. "Training" means iteratively adjusting numeric parameters so the function makes fewer mistakes on the labeled examples.

That's it. The complications come from *which* function shape (linear regression vs random forest vs neural network), *which* data (clean vs messy), and *which* objective (accuracy vs precision vs recall vs business KPI).

## The three concepts to actually internalise

### 1. Train / validate / test

You never evaluate a model on the data it learned from. You split data into three sets:
- **Train** — the model fits these.
- **Validate** — you tune hyperparameters by checking accuracy here.
- **Test** — you touch this exactly *once*, at the very end, to report the number you'll show in the meeting.

If you mix them up ("data leakage"), you'll think your model is great and it'll fail in production. This is the #1 mistake in junior ML work.

### 2. Overfitting vs underfitting

- **Underfit:** model is too simple. Bad on train AND test. (Think: drawing a straight line through a curvy dataset.)
- **Overfit:** model memorised the training data. Great on train, bad on test. (Think: drawing a wiggly line through every single point.)

The whole game is finding the middle. Common levers: more data, simpler model, regularisation, dropout.

### 3. Accuracy is rarely what you actually want

If your dataset is 99% spam-not-spam and 1% spam, a model that says "never spam" is **99% accurate** and useless.

What you actually care about depends on the cost of being wrong:
- Email spam → optimise **precision** (don't block real emails).
- Cancer screening → optimise **recall** (don't miss real cancer).
- Fraud detection → optimise the **dollar cost** of false positives vs false negatives.

Pick the metric *before* you start training. Otherwise you'll optimise the wrong thing.

## What to ask the data scientist

When someone shows you a model, four questions:

1. **"What's the baseline?"** ("My model is 85% accurate" — vs what? A coin flip? The previous model? The simplest possible heuristic?)
2. **"How was the train/test split?"** (Especially: is there time-leakage? If you're predicting next month's sales, your test set must be data the model never saw, in time.)
3. **"What's the failure mode?"** (When this model is wrong, what does it look like? Loud and obvious, or silent and biased?)
4. **"How will this drift?"** (Models age. The data shifts. When does this need retraining?)

Asking these questions positions you as a senior collaborator, even if you've never built a model.

diagrams.caption: Supervised learning lifecycle — what "training" actually means
diagrams.source:
graph LR
    Data[Labeled examples] --> Split{Train Val Test}
    Split -->|fit on| Train[Train set]
    Split -->|tune on| Val[Validation set]
    Split -->|measure on| Test[Test set]
    Train --> Model[Model parameters]
    Val --> Model
    Model --> Predict[Predictions on new data]
    Test --> Report[Honest accuracy number]
    Predict --> Drift{Distribution drift}
    Drift -->|yes| Retrain[Retrain]
    Drift -->|no| Keep[Keep using]
    style Data fill:#dbeafe
    style Test fill:#fde68a
    style Drift fill:#fee2e2
    style Report fill:#dcfce7

tutorOpener: Have you used or integrated an ML model in any project? Tell me what it did, and we'll work through what "trained" actually meant in that context.
